repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem:
Non-trivial to write output of vim.net.request to buffer. Currently requires extra
code in `runtime/plugin/net.lua` which can't be reused by others.

```lua
vim.net.request('https://neovim.io', {}, vim.schedule_wrap(function(err, res)
  if not err then
    local buf = vim.api.nvim_create_buf(true, false)
    if res then
      local lines = vim.split(res.body, '\n', { plain = true })
      vim.api.nvim_buf_set_lines(buf, 0, -1, true, lines)
    end
  end
end))
```

Solution:
Accept an optional `outbuf` argument to indicate the buffer to write output
to, similar to `outpath`.

```lua
vim.net.request('https://neovim.io', { outbuf = buf })
```

---

It would be really nice to do this in streaming manner, but I don't think `vim.system` currently supports this? Anyone an idea if this would be possible?

---

Fixes a couple follow-ups mentioned in #34140:
- [Make plugin/net.lua smaller](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/34140#discussion_r2203512183)
- [Option to cancel requests](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/34140#discussion_r2173754322). @lewis6991 care to review this? I simply added the return value proposed by @tampueroc.
- [vim.net.request.Opts class](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/34140#discussion_r2203509803)
- [vim.validate single calls](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/34140#discussion_r2203509951)
- [Use `(''):format(...)` instead of `..`](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/34140#discussion_r2203513647)

@@ -3946,27 +3946,58 @@ Lua module: vim.net                                                  *vim.net*
 vim.net.request({url}, {opts}, {on_response})              *vim.net.request()*
     Makes an HTTP GET request to the given URL (asynchronous).
 
-    This function operates in one mode:
-    • Asynchronous (non-blocking): Returns immediately and passes the response
-      object to the provided `on_response` handler on completion.
+    This function is asynchronous (non-blocking), returning immediately and
+    passing the response object to the optional `on_response` handler on
+    completion.
+
+    Examples: >lua
+        -- Write to response to file
+        vim.net.request('https://neovim.io/charter/', {
+          outpath = 'vision.html',
+        })
+
+        -- Process the response
+        vim.net.request(
+          'https://api.github.com/repos/neovim/neovim',
+          {},
+          function (err, res)
+            if err then return end
+            local stars = vim.json.decode(res.body).stargazers_count
+            vim.print(('Neovim currently has %d stars'):format(stars))
+          end
+        )
+
+        -- Write to both file and current buffer, but cancel it
+        local job = vim.net.request('https://neovim.io/charter/', {
+          outpath = 'vision.html',
+          outbuf = 0,
+        })
+        job:cancel()
+<
 
     Parameters: ~
       • {url}          (`string`) The URL for the request.

-                       • `verbose` (boolean|nil): Enables verbose output.
-                       • `retry` (integer|nil): Number of retries on transient

+                       • {verbose} (`boolean?`) Enables verbose output.
+                       • {retry} (`integer?`) Number of retries on transient
                          failures (default: 3).

+                         body to. If set, the `body` value in the Response
+                         Object will be `true` instead of the response body.
+      • {on_response}  (`fun(err: string?, response: { body: string|boolean }?)?`)
                        Callback invoked on request completion. The `body`
                        field in the response object contains the raw response

-                       failure, or (nil, { body = string|boolean }) on

I don't actually know if there is a finalized pattern for passing an error to a callback, but I think usually err is passed as the first arg. paging @lewis6991 @mfussenegger 

related?: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/34140#discussion_r2173754322

comment_data.body: `output=ev.buf` works with `BufReadCmd` to `:edit <url>` (set a new buffer with its contents). `FileReadCmd` is used with `:read <url>` to insert the url contents into the current buffer. (add content to current buffer). So we can't "overwrite" the buf contents using `outbuf`.

+    -- FileReadCmd (:read) inserts in the current buffer. We mimic that
+    -- behavior by writing the output to a scratch buffer and then copying it
+    -- to the current buffer.

comment_data.body:
If `on_response` is not given, I think we want that to have synchronous behavior. So I'm not sure it's a good idea to make it optional until we have that? Or maybe it doesn't matter since `vim.async` would allow the caller to opt-in to synchronous behavior. @lewis6991 wdyt

(It's easy for caller to pass an empty `function()end` if they don't care about the result.)

comment_regular_data.body:
Nice to see this. Buffers should be a ubiquitous concept accepted by any function that can write output. Similar to how URIs should be accepted wherever a filepath is accepted.

Eventually we should think about how to make this more pervasive, but this is a good step for now, thanks!

> Buffers should be a ubiquitous concept accepted by any function that can write output.

Yes, I agree. Initially also thought about changing `outpath`/`outbuf` to simply `out` that can be an integer (buffer) or string (filepath), but didn't do it bc I didn't see the pattern anywhere else. Would be nice though.
